text: Hi, this is Dr. Augustino Cervone. I produced this video for patient education to help explain what it's like to perform a lysis of adhesions.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to set up for the procedure on a patient with an abdominal hernia, preparing to perform adhesiolysis and hernia repair.
The surgeon begins by carefully dissecting and removing scar tissue from the abdominal cavity, which is a result of previous surgeries or inflammation.

The camera is introduced into the abdomen, revealing normal intestinal contents. The anterior abdominal wall shows scarring from previous surgery, with the omentum and intestines suspended by the scar tissue.
The surgeon uses fine scissors and occasional cautery to carefully dissect through adhesions, freeing the anterior abdominal wall from scar tissue.

The nice advantage of the robot is that I am peering into the abdomen using high definition imagery as well as the images are three dimensional. So each of my moves are intentional. As much as I can't feel what

tension I'm putting on tissue, it becomes a sense of understanding how the tissue surrounding what you're dissecting reacts. So you get an impression of how much pulling or tugging is enough, too little, or too much.

The surgeon releases adhesions using cautery, carefully navigating through fat tissue. As the dissection approaches the intestines, the surgeon proceeds with caution to avoid injury, highlighting the importance of slow and deliberate movements.

The surgeon uses robotic assistance to meticulously dissect individual strands of scar tissue within the abdomen, demonstrating the precision and steadiness of the robotic instruments during the adhesiolysis procedure.

I will also say that as much as we expect there to be adhesions in a patient who has had previous operations, certainly somebody with an abdominal wall hernia, we would expect adhesions. We can never tell without actually looking in there what's involved, what's going to be involved, how much time is needed, and what's to be expected. No matter if somebody has had CAT scans, ultrasounds, other studies that didn't involve us being in the abdomen, it's impossible to tell beforehand. And as you can see, this is a process that can take a period of time.

And certainly, it can take longer than the actual repair of the hernia. And from a surgeon's perspective, the longer time we're in the operating room performing any portion of an operation, the more fatigue that we encounter. As you saw at the beginning of this video, in performing robotic surgery, the surgeon sits behind a console. This limits the amount of fatigue.

I would also add that comfort of the surgeon potentially translates into better outcomes for the patient. And my reason for saying that is that when performing robotic surgery, the surgeon is relatively comfortable behind the console. And taking it to a sports analogy, the surgeon gets into his or her zone, so to speak, when performing robotics.
Well, I hope you've enjoyed my little video on license of adhesions, and hopefully it's created some clarity.
